candidate_problems.text: How can the conjecture be operationalized into measurable variables that distinguish 'distributed computation' from 'concentrated computation' in real economies, and do those measures predict broad prosperity better than rival explanations such as skill-biased technical change, capital deepening, or institutional quality?

What are the actual causal mechanisms by which control over recursive learning loops translates into durable concentration of gains, and under what conditions can redistribution, regulation, or collective institutions offset that concentration without also degrading the adaptive capacity the conjecture treats as central?

What system-level tradeoff exists between decentralizing compute-and-adapt capacity and preserving the scale advantages of centralized platforms, data aggregation, and standardization; specifically, when does centralization increase total innovation enough to outweigh its tendency to narrow participation in feedback loops?
